topics.title: Primitive Types & Variables
topics.description: Learn Java's built-in primitive types and how to declare and initialise variables.

lessons.title: Strongly typed language
lessons.body: Java is statically typed — every variable must be declared with a type before use. The type can never change.

lessons.title: Integer and floating-point types
lessons.body: int holds 32-bit integers. long holds 64-bit integers. double is the default floating-point type.

lessons.title: char and boolean
lessons.body: char holds a single Unicode character (use single quotes). boolean holds true or false.

lessons.title: String (not a primitive)
lessons.body: String is a class, not a primitive. Use double quotes. Strings are immutable — operations return new strings.

topics.title: Classes & Objects
topics.description: Understand Java's object-oriented fundamentals: classes, fields, methods, and constructors.

lessons.title: Defining a class
lessons.body: A class is a blueprint for objects. It contains fields (data) and methods (behaviour).

lessons.title: Constructors
lessons.body: A constructor initialises a new object. It has the same name as the class and no return type.

lessons.title: Methods
lessons.body: Methods define what an object can do. Use the return type void when nothing is returned.

lessons.title: Creating objects
lessons.body: Use the new keyword to create an instance of a class.
lessons.code:
Dog rex = new Dog("Rex", 3);
rex.bark();  // Rex says: Woof!

topics.title: Generics & Collections
topics.description: Use Java generics and the Collections framework for type-safe, reusable data structures.

lessons.title: Why generics?
lessons.body: Before generics, collections stored Object and required casting. Generics enforce the element type at compile time.

lessons.title: Generic class syntax
lessons.body: Declare a type parameter in angle brackets. T is the conventional name for a single type parameter.

lessons.title: Wildcards
lessons.body: ? extends T means any subtype of T (read-only use). ? super T means any supertype of T (write use).

lessons.title: Common collections
lessons.body: ArrayList is a resizable array. HashMap is a key-value store. HashSet is an unordered set of unique elements.

topics.title: Streams & Functional Interfaces
topics.description: Process collections declaratively using Java Streams and lambda expressions.

lessons.title: What is a Stream?
lessons.body: A Stream is a sequence of elements supporting sequential and parallel aggregate operations. Streams don't store data — they process it lazily.

lessons.title: Intermediate operations
lessons.body: filter, map, and sorted are lazy — they build a pipeline but don't execute until a terminal operation is called.

lessons.title: Terminal operations
lessons.body: collect, forEach, reduce, and count trigger the pipeline. A stream can only be consumed once.

lessons.title: Parallel streams
lessons.body: Use parallelStream() to process elements concurrently. Effective for large, independent datasets — but adds overhead for small ones.
